pattern: Because a number that reads the same forwards and backwards in one base is a palindrome purely by construction, it is far cheaper to build palindromic candidates digit-by-digit in increasing order and test the other base than to sweep every integer and test both bases.
hints: Scanning every integer and checking both bases is too slow once the target values get large; instead generate base-10 palindromes directly, in increasing numeric order, by choosing only their first half of digits and mirroring it (dropping the middle digit for an odd total length).

For each base-10 palindrome candidate, convert it to base k with repeated division and modulo, most significant digit last, then reverse the digits, and check whether that base-k digit sequence is itself a palindrome.

Walk candidates length by length (1-digit palindromes, then 2-digit, then 3-digit, ...); every palindrome of a shorter length is smaller than every palindrome of a longer length, so this order is already the increasing order you need -- keep a running sum and stop the moment you have collected n qualifying values.

edge_inputs.description: smallest possible n, trivially just the number 1
smallest base in range with a small n
largest base in range with the smallest n
a mid-size base with a mid-size n
a base where several small values are trivial single-digit k-mirror numbers in both bases before larger multi-digit ones appear
largest base in range with the largest n
smallest base in range with the largest n
a base and n combination requiring several increasing digit-lengths before enough qualifying values are found

stress.description: random valid (k, n) pairs sampled across the whole small, finite domain
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    k = rng.randint(2, 9)
    n = rng.randint(1, 30)
    return [k, n]

stress.description: a second batch of random valid (k, n) pairs, biased toward larger n to add confidence at the more expensive end of the domain
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    k = rng.randint(2, 9)
    n = rng.randint(15, 30)
    return [k, n]

complexity.time: O(P * L) where P is the number of base-10 palindromes examined and L is the digit length of each
